title: Greater than or equal to operator (>=) in Java

content.problemStatement:
In Java, the **greater than or equal to (`>=`) operator** is used to compare two primitive values. It checks whether the value on the left side is greater than or equal to the value on the right side and returns a boolean result (`true` or `false`).

---

**Comparing Integers**
The `>=` operator can be used to compare integer values:

```java
int num1 = 20; // num1 holds an integer value of 20
int num2 = 15; // num2 holds an integer value of 15
System.out.println(num1 >= num2);  // Outputs: true

int num3 = 10; // num3 holds an integer value of 10
int num4 = 10; // num4 holds an integer value of 10
System.out.println(num3 >= num4);  // Outputs: true
```

**Comparing Floating-Point Numbers**
The `>=` operator also works with floating-point numbers (`double` and `float`):

```java
double temperature1 = 36.5; // temperature1 holds a double value of 36.5
double temperature2 = 37.0; // temperature2 holds a double value of 37.0
System.out.println(temperature1 >= temperature2);  // Outputs: false
```

**Comparing Characters**
Since characters (`char`) are internally represented as numeric Unicode values, they can be compared using `>=`:

```java
char gradeA = 'A'; // gradeA holds the character 'A' (Unicode 65)
char gradeB = 'B'; // gradeB holds the character 'B' (Unicode 66)
System.out.println(gradeA >= gradeB);  // Outputs: false
```
